narrative: As a Black male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, looking at factors like grades, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.3 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 23 (well under the 36.7 average), middle family income bracket, full-time student status, and school deciles of 5th in Year 1 and Year 3 grades—and predicted a 45% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try. This elevated risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain their standards. The three biggest factors driving this prediction were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your LSAT score, and your undergraduate GPA, in that order of influence. Your Year 3 decile of 5—right around the average of 5.53—actually worked in your favor, nudging the model toward seeing you as more likely to pass compared to students with weaker end-of-undergrad performance. On the other hand, your LSAT score had the strongest push toward predicting failure, as it stands out as notably low against the experiences of most admitted students who score much higher. Your undergraduate GPA also pulled the prediction toward failure, reinforcing concerns since it's below the typical level that correlates with bar success. While your full-time status aligns with the vast majority of students (over 92%), and your middle-income background is common, they didn't outweigh these key academic signals. Overall, your profile compares to typical students by having solid relative school rankings but lagging significantly on the standardized metrics that the model weighs heavily for bar passage. This isn't a judgment on your potential or the challenges you may have faced as a Black man navigating these systems—many overcome similar hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your GPA through post-bac courses or a master's program, and gathering strong letters highlighting your resilience and non-academic strengths. Reapplying after addressing these could shift the model's view dramatically, opening doors at schools that value diverse perspectives like yours. Keep pushing; your story is far from over.
